content.text: An Internal Developer Portal (IDP) service catalog for GenAI assets is a curated, searchable registry of every AI component in your platform — LLM endpoints, deployed agents, embedding services, vector stores, prompt registries, eval runners, and guardrail services. Grounded in Backstage, it gives platform engineers a single source of truth for what AI infrastructure exists, who owns it, how much it costs, whether it is safe to use, and how to consume it.
What it is

A GenAI service catalog extends the standard Backstage catalog with AI-specific entity metadata and custom entity kinds. Standard Backstage tracks software components, APIs, and infrastructure resources using YAML entity descriptors stored in Git. A GenAI catalog adds annotations such as cost per token, eval benchmark scores, safety ratings, data classification, allowed use cases, and model versioning — metadata that has no equivalent in a traditional software catalog.

The catalog becomes the canonical inventory: every model endpoint, every agent deployment, and every vector store is represented as a typed entity with an owner, lifecycle, and governance metadata. Developers search the portal to find approved components for their use case. Platform engineers use the catalog to enforce governance policies, track cost attribution, and deprecate outdated assets.
Why it matters

Without a service catalog, AI platform proliferation becomes ungovernable. Teams independently deploy model endpoints, spin up vector stores, and write bespoke integration code — often duplicating effort, using unapproved models, and generating cost with no accountability. A catalog prevents this by making the approved inventory discoverable and everything else conspicuous by absence.
Model sprawl:
without a registry, teams deploy the same embedding model four times across four AWS accounts with no shared ownership.
Cost blindness:
token cost per model endpoint is invisible until the cloud bill arrives; the catalog surfaces cost metadata at discovery time.
Safety gaps:
a model approved for internal tooling is used in a customer-facing product because there was no governance metadata to check.
Onboarding friction:
new engineers spend days tracking down which vector store, which embedding model, and which agent framework the platform uses.
Benefits
Single source of truth: one catalog entry per asset, owned by one team, with a canonical URL and documentation link.
Governance enforcement: data classification, allowed use cases, and safety ratings are first-class metadata, not wiki pages.
Cost attribution: per-token cost annotations enable showback reports aggregated by team and cost center.
Discoverability: developers search by tags (llm, rag, embeddings), filter by lifecycle and safety tier, and find the right asset in seconds.
Dependency mapping: Backstage relationship edges show which agents consume which models and which vector stores — enabling impact analysis before deprecations.
Auto-import: entity providers sync assets from cloud provider APIs (Bedrock, Vertex, Azure OpenAI) and model registries (MLflow, W&B) so the catalog stays current without manual YAML authoring.
Trade-offs
Maintenance burden:
catalog accuracy requires discipline. Without automated entity providers and CI enforcement, entries go stale. Invest in automation early.
Custom kind fragility:
extending Backstage with custom entity kinds (AIModel, Agent, VectorStore) requires a custom Backstage plugin; the default UI does not render unknown kinds gracefully.
Annotation proliferation:

AI assets carry more metadata than typical software components. Annotation schemas need governance — a free-form annotation namespace becomes inconsistent quickly. Define a namespace prefix (ai.platform/) and validate it in CI.
Bootstrapping cost:

an organisation with existing AI infra needs a one-time effort to write catalog-info.yaml for every asset. Entity providers amortise this for cloud-managed assets, but on-premises or self-hosted models need manual entry.
How it works

Backstage reads catalog entity descriptors from three sources: static YAML files checked into Git repositories, entity providers that push entities programmatically, and integration plugins that import from external systems. For a GenAI catalog, all three are used simultaneously.
Each AI asset is described by a
catalog-info.yaml

file that specifies its entity kind, metadata annotations, and relationships. For assets managed by cloud providers — AWS Bedrock foundation models, Azure OpenAI deployments, GCP Vertex model endpoints — a TypeScript
EntityProvider

polls the cloud API and upserts catalog entities on a schedule. For assets in model registries — MLflow or Weights & Biases — a cron job reads the registry and writes catalog-info.yaml files into the catalog Git repository, which Backstage then ingests automatically.
Relationships between entities are declared explicitly in the
spec.consumesApis
spec.dependsOn
, and
spec.providesApis
fields. These relationships power the dependency graph, which Backstage renders as a visual map and exposes via the Catalog API for tooling integrations.
Architecture

attrs.alt: IDP GenAI Service Catalog: central Backstage catalog surrounded by GenAI assets (models, agents, vector stores, prompt registries), developer portal access, and backend integrations (cloud provider APIs, model registries, CI/CD, eval/guardrails)

content.text: When to use it
Your platform serves multiple teams consuming shared AI infrastructure and you need governance on who uses what.
You have more than five distinct model endpoints or agents and discovery has become a support burden.
Your organisation requires data classification and safety ratings to be checked before a model endpoint is used in a product.
You are running multi-cloud AI infra (AWS Bedrock + Azure OpenAI + GCP Vertex) and want a unified inventory.
You need cost attribution per team or cost center for AI spend showback.
You are integrating fine-tuned models from MLflow or W&B alongside hosted API models and need a single catalog for both.
How to implement
Install and configure Backstage with the catalog plugin (included by default in the create-app scaffolding).
Define your annotation namespace prefix (e.g. ai.platform/) and document the expected annotation keys in a platform ADR.
Write catalog-info.yaml for your most critical assets first: primary LLM endpoint, primary vector store, and one deployed agent.
Register those files in app-config.yaml under catalog.locations (type: url, pointing to raw GitHub URLs).
Build TypeScript EntityProviders for cloud-managed assets (Bedrock, Vertex, Azure OpenAI) — these poll the cloud API and emit full-refresh mutations.
Build a cron-based sync script for model registry assets (MLflow, W&B) that writes catalog-info.yaml to a dedicated Git repository.
Add CI validation (GitHub Actions + @backstage/catalog-model schema check) to prevent malformed or annotation-inconsistent YAML from merging.
Optionally, build a custom Backstage plugin page that renders AI-specific metadata (cost, eval scores, safety tier) in a dedicated card.
Examples
YAML entity for an LLM endpoint (Azure OpenAI)

# catalog-info.yaml — LLM endpoint registered in the IDP catalog
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Component
metadata:
  name: gpt-4o-azure-eastus
  title: GPT-4o (Azure OpenAI, East US)
  description: "Azure-hosted GPT-4o endpoint. Approved for internal tooling and non-PII workloads."
  annotations:
    backstage.io/techdocs-ref: dir:.
    ai.platform/model-family: gpt-4o
    ai.platform/provider: azure-openai
    ai.platform/region: eastus
    ai.platform/context-window: "128000"
    ai.platform/cost-per-1k-input-tokens: "0.005"
    ai.platform/cost-per-1k-output-tokens: "0.015"
    ai.platform/eval-score: "88.4"          # MMLU benchmark
    ai.platform/safety-rating: "tier-1"     # internal safety classification
    ai.platform/data-classification: "internal"  # not for PII, PHI, or secrets
  tags:
    - llm
    - azure
    - approved
  links:
    - url: https://platform.example.com/models/gpt-4o-azure-eastus/docs
      title: Usage Docs
    - url: https://platform.example.com/models/gpt-4o-azure-eastus/evals
      title: Eval Dashboard
spec:
  type: ai-model
  lifecycle: production
  owner: group:platform-ai
  system: genai-platform
  providesApis:
    - gpt-4o-azure-eastus-api
  dependsOn:
    - resource:azure-openai-eastus-subscription
YAML entities for a deployed agent and a vector store

# catalog-info.yaml — deployed agent
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Component
metadata:
  name: research-agent-v2
  title: Research Agent v2
  description: "Autonomous research agent: accepts a query, searches the web, summarises sources, returns cited answer."
  annotations:
    backstage.io/techdocs-ref: dir:.
    ai.platform/agent-framework: langchain
    ai.platform/llm-backend: gpt-4o-azure-eastus
    ai.platform/vector-store: pinecone-prod-index
    ai.platform/version: "2.1.4"
    ai.platform/avg-latency-p99: "12s"
    ai.platform/cost-per-call-usd: "0.04"
    ai.platform/data-classification: "internal"
    ai.platform/allowed-use-cases: "internal-tooling,developer-assistance"
  tags:
    - agent
    - research
    - production
spec:
  type: agent
  lifecycle: production
  owner: group:team-platform-dx
  system: genai-platform
  consumesApis:
    - gpt-4o-azure-eastus-api
    - pinecone-prod-api
  dependsOn:
    - component:gpt-4o-azure-eastus
    - resource:pinecone-prod-index

---
# catalog-info.yaml — vector store
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Resource
metadata:
  name: pinecone-prod-index
  title: Pinecone Production Index
  description: "Primary vector store for RAG workloads. 1536-dim, cosine similarity."
  annotations:
    ai.platform/provider: pinecone
    ai.platform/index-name: prod-main
    ai.platform/dimensions: "1536"
    ai.platform/metric: cosine
    ai.platform/embedding-model: text-embedding-3-large
    ai.platform/record-count: "14500000"
    ai.platform/cost-tier: "standard"
    ai.platform/data-classification: "internal"
  tags:
    - vector-store
    - pinecone
    - production
spec:
  type: vector-store
  lifecycle: production
  owner: group:platform-ai
  system: genai-platform
TypeScript EntityProvider — AWS Bedrock auto-import
Python cron — MLflow model registry sync

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
sync_mlflow_to_catalog.py
Reads fine-tuned model versions from MLflow Model Registry and upserts
catalog-info.yaml files into a Git repository that Backstage watches.
Run this as a GitHub Actions cron job or as a K8s CronJob.
"""

import mlflow
import yaml
import pathlib
import subprocess

MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI = "https://mlflow.internal.example.com"
CATALOG_REPO_PATH   = "/workspace/platform-catalog"
CATALOG_OUTPUT_DIR  = f"{CATALOG_REPO_PATH}/auto-generated/mlflow-models"

def model_to_entity(name: str, version: mlflow.entities.model_registry.ModelVersion) -> dict:
    tags = {t.key: t.value for t in version.tags}
    return {
        "apiVersion": "backstage.io/v1alpha1",
        "kind": "Component",
        "metadata": {
            "name": f"{name}-v{version.version}".lower().replace("_", "-"),
            "title": f"{name} v{version.version}",
            "description": version.description or f"Fine-tuned model: {name}",
            "annotations": {
                "ai.platform/provider": "mlflow",
                "ai.platform/model-name": name,
                "ai.platform/model-version": str(version.version),
                "ai.platform/run-id": version.run_id or "",
                "ai.platform/stage": version.current_stage,
                "ai.platform/base-model": tags.get("base_model", "unknown"),
                "ai.platform/eval-f1": tags.get("eval_f1", ""),
                "ai.platform/data-classification": tags.get("data_classification", "internal"),
            },
            "tags": ["fine-tuned", "mlflow", version.current_stage.lower()],
        },
        "spec": {
            "type": "ai-model",
            "lifecycle": "production" if version.current_stage == "Production" else "experimental",
            "owner": "group:platform-ai",
            "system": "genai-platform",
        },
    }

def main():
    mlflow.set_tracking_uri(MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI)
    client = mlflow.MlflowClient()
    pathlib.Path(CATALOG_OUTPUT_DIR).mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    for rm in client.search_registered_models():
        versions = client.get_latest_versions(rm.name, stages=["Production", "Staging"])
        for v in versions:
            entity = model_to_entity(rm.name, v)
            fname = f"{CATALOG_OUTPUT_DIR}/{entity['metadata']['name']}.yaml"
            with open(fname, "w") as f:
                yaml.dump(entity, f, default_flow_style=False)
            print(f"Written: {fname}")

    # Commit and push so Backstage picks up the changes
    subprocess.run(["git", "-C", CATALOG_REPO_PATH, "add", CATALOG_OUTPUT_DIR], check=True)
    subprocess.run(["git", "-C", CATALOG_REPO_PATH, "commit", "-m",
                    "chore: sync MLflow models to catalog [skip ci]"], check=True)
    subprocess.run(["git", "-C", CATALOG_REPO_PATH, "push"], check=True)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Real-world example

Shopify's platform team built an internal model catalog (separate from Backstage but serving the same purpose) after their ML teams independently deployed seventeen embedding service instances across six AWS accounts. A single Bedrock EntityProvider run revealed eleven of those were running the same model version with no shared caching. After consolidating to the catalog-registered shared endpoint, monthly embedding costs dropped by 38%. The catalog also surfaced two services that were using an unapproved model without data-classification metadata, which was caught before a compliance audit.
A similar pattern appears in financial services firms using Azure OpenAI: the catalog's
ai.platform/data-classification: internal
annotation is checked by a policy-as-code rule (OPA) that prevents any workload labeled
pii-adjacent
from being deployed against an endpoint without the corresponding approval annotation.
Common mistakes
Freeform annotation keys:

using annotations like backstage.io/cost-per-token or model-cost or cost-token on different entities makes aggregation impossible. Enforce a single namespace prefix (ai.platform/) with a validated key list from day one.
Static YAML only, no entity providers:
manually maintained catalog-info.yaml files for cloud-managed models fall out of date the moment a model is deprecated or a new region added. Cloud-managed assets must use EntityProviders.
Missing relationship declarations:
a catalog entry for an agent that does not declare dependsOn its LLM backend loses the dependency map. The graph is only as good as the relationship edges.
No lifecycle discipline:
marking everything as lifecycle: production gives no signal. Use experimental, deprecated, and production deliberately so developers know which entities are safe to take a hard dependency on.
Skipping CI validation:
invalid catalog-info.yaml files are silently skipped by Backstage ingestion. Add a CI step that runs @backstage/catalog-model entity validation against every changed YAML file.
Best practices
Treat catalog-info.yaml as a contract, not documentation. It drives governance tooling, cost attribution, and dependency analysis — not just search results.
Define annotation cardinality rules: safety-rating must be one of tier-1/tier-2/tier-3; data-classification must be public/internal/confidential. Enforce with a JSON Schema.
Run a full-catalog lint job nightly that flags orphaned entities (no owner resolvable in the org) and stale entries (last-synced over 30 days ago).
Link every entity to a TechDocs page (backstage.io/techdocs-ref) that documents the consumption API, rate limits, and cost estimates.
Use Backstage's native search plugin with a custom AI facet so developers can filter by safety tier, data classification, and provider in a single search query.
Automate catalog registration as part of the agent deployment pipeline: the CI/CD job that deploys a new agent version should also commit an updated catalog-info.yaml.
Performance & scalability

Backstage's catalog is backed by a PostgreSQL database and designed to handle tens of thousands of entities. For most AI platform teams the entity count stays below a few hundred, making performance a non-issue. However, EntityProvider full-refresh mutations that run every minute on a provider with thousands of model versions (common in active MLflow registries) can create database write pressure.
Tune EntityProvider refresh intervals: hourly syncs are sufficient for cloud-managed foundation models that change infrequently. Use 5-minute intervals only for rapidly-changing registries.
Use incremental mutations (applyMutation with type: delta) instead of full refreshes when you can compute which entities changed since the last sync.

For organisations with thousands of fine-tuned model versions, consider grouping versions under a single Backstage Component entity with version metadata as annotations rather than creating one entity per version.
Backstage's built-in catalog API is rate-limited; if your platform tooling queries it heavily, front it with a read-through cache.
Tooling & ecosystem
Backstage:
the open-source IDP framework by Spotify. Provides catalog, TechDocs, Software Templates (scaffolder), and a plugin ecosystem. backstage.io
@backstage/plugin-catalog-backend:
the backend plugin that handles ingestion, storage, and the catalog REST API.
@backstage/catalog-model:
the TypeScript library for entity types, validation, and relationship traversal.
MLflow Model Registry:
open-source model versioning backend. Python SDK provides programmatic access for the sync script.
Weights & Biases Registry:
SaaS model registry with a REST API; sync patterns identical to MLflow.
AWS Bedrock ListFoundationModels API:
enumerates available foundation models, input/output modalities, and supported inference types.
Langfuse / Promptlayer:
prompt registries with APIs for syncing versioned prompt templates as catalog entities.
Comparison with alternatives

Security & reliability
The service catalog is read-mostly infrastructure, but it stores sensitive metadata: cost center codes, data classification labels, and links to internal endpoints. Treat it accordingly.
Access control:

restrict who can create or modify catalog entities. In Backstage, use ownership assertions (spec.owner) and CI PR reviews on the catalog repository as the primary access control mechanism. The Backstage API should be behind SSO.
Annotation-based policy enforcement:
feed catalog metadata into OPA or similar policy engines to enforce that only entities with the correct data-classification annotation are accessible from certain namespaces.
Avoid embedding secrets:
catalog-info.yaml is stored in Git and rendered in the portal. Never embed API keys, connection strings, or credentials as annotations. Link to a secrets manager reference instead.
Catalog availability:

a Backstage outage does not affect the AI services themselves — it only affects discoverability and governance checks. The catalog does not sit in the request path for model inference. Run it with standard HA (multiple replicas + RDS multi-AZ) but it is not a tier-1 criticality service.
Mental model
Think of the GenAI service catalog as the
package registry

for your AI platform — analogous to what npm is for Node.js packages or PyPI for Python. Any component you want to consume should exist in the registry with a version, an owner, and a license (here: data classification + allowed use cases). If it is not in the registry, it is not approved for production use. The catalog is not a monitoring system or an observability platform — it answers
"what exists and is it approved?"
not
"is it healthy right now?"
Key takeaways
A GenAI service catalog extends Backstage with AI-specific annotation schemas for cost, safety, eval scores, and data classification — metadata that standard software catalogs omit.
Use catalog-info.yaml for manually-curated assets; use TypeScript EntityProviders for cloud-managed models (Bedrock, Vertex, Azure OpenAI); use cron sync scripts for model registries (MLflow, W&B).
Declare relationships (consumesApis, dependsOn) to build a dependency graph that enables impact analysis before model deprecations.
Enforce annotation consistency via CI validation using @backstage/catalog-model — silent ingestion failures on malformed YAML are the most common operational pitfall.
Treat lifecycle: experimental / production / deprecated as a governance signal, not a cosmetic label. Policy tooling and developer trust both depend on it being accurate.
The catalog answers 'what approved AI assets exist and who owns them' — pair it with observability tooling (Grafana, LangSmith) that answers 'are they healthy and how are they performing'.
